evaluator_exp-2025-11-30__vs__evaluator_.summary: Both artifacts describe the same AI Scientist-v2 system and experimental results, with identical core findings but different perspectives and emphasis. They agree on the fundamental achievement of first AI-generated paper acceptance at peer review, but differ in how they frame the system's capabilities and limitations.

full_result.summary: Both artifacts describe the same AI Scientist-v2 system and experimental results, with identical core findings but different perspectives and emphasis. They agree on the fundamental achievement of first AI-generated paper acceptance at peer review, but differ in how they frame the system's capabilities and limitations.

agreements.topic: First AI-generated paper acceptance at peer review
agreements.description: Both confirm that one paper (Compositional Regularization) was accepted at ICLR 2025 ICBINB Workshop with scores 6, 7, 6, representing the first documented case of AI-generated research passing peer review
Both report 1 out of 3 papers accepted, with similar confidence intervals acknowledging the small sample size makes statistical conclusions difficult

agreements.topic: Template-free operation as improvement over v1
agreements.description: Both emphasize that the system eliminates domain-specific template dependency that limited AI Scientist v1, enabling broader domain applicability

agreements.topic: Tree search and VLM integration as key innovations
agreements.description: Both identify the four-stage tree search methodology and vision-language model feedback as core technical contributions

agreements.topic: Workshop-level target rather than main conference
agreements.description: Both acknowledge this was targeted at workshop acceptance rather than main conference publication

contradictions.artifact_a_claim: Explicitly lists human selection as failure cases and limitations: 'System requires human selection of ideas (3 from ~40 generated)' and 'System requires human selection of best manuscript from multiple runs'
contradictions.artifact_b_claim: Describes system as 'autonomous' and 'end-to-end' without prominently featuring human selection as limitations
contradictions.possible_explanation: Artifact A takes a more critical stance on autonomy claims, while Artifact B emphasizes the system's capabilities

contradictions.topic: Evidence strength for system superiority claims
contradictions.artifact_a_claim: Rates claim about surpassing v1 as 'contested' with confidence 0.4, noting lack of direct experimental comparison
contradictions.artifact_b_claim: Rates template elimination claim as 'established' with confidence 0.9, presenting it as definitively proven
contradictions.possible_explanation: Different interpretation of what constitutes sufficient evidence for improvement claims

unique_to_a: Detailed failure case analysis with specific rejection reasons
Explicit confidence intervals with numerical bounds (1.7% to 79.1%)
Critical assessment of autonomous capability claims
Identification of confounding factors in causal relationships
unique_to_b: Hardware requirements specification (NVIDIA GPU with CUDA)
Comparison to workshop baseline acceptance rates (60-70%)
More detailed hyperparameter specifications for each stage
complementary: Artifact A provides more detailed statistical analysis while Artifact B adds practical considerations like cost and hardware
A offers critical evaluation of autonomy claims while B provides operational details
A focuses on experimental rigor and limitations while B emphasizes technical architecture and feasibility
Together they provide both technical validation (A) and practical deployment considerations (B)
methodology_differences: Artifact A reports 'num_runs': 3 while Artifact B reports 'num_runs': 1, suggesting different experimental perspectives
Different hyperparameter naming conventions (stage_1_nodes vs stage_1_iterations)
A includes baseline comparison field as empty while B explicitly lists 'AI Scientist v1' as baseline
full_result.recommendation: Use both artifacts together for comprehensive understanding. Artifact A provides essential critical analysis of the system's limitations and statistical rigor needed for scientific evaluation. Artifact B provides practical implementation details and cost considerations important for deployment. The contradictions highlight the need to carefully examine autonomy claims and distinguish between technical capability and true end-to-end automation.

evaluator_exp-2025-11-30__vs__evaluator_.summary: These artifacts investigate fundamentally different aspects of automated AI research. Artifact A evaluates an end-to-end system (AI Scientist-v2) for generating complete research papers from ideas to peer-reviewed publication, achieving the first documented AI-generated paper acceptance. Artifact B analyzes a specific component of autonomous research systems - ablation planning via causal reasoning - and finds that d-separation over-prunes valid ablations, making it unsuitable for experimental design.

full_result.summary: These artifacts investigate fundamentally different aspects of automated AI research. Artifact A evaluates an end-to-end system (AI Scientist-v2) for generating complete research papers from ideas to peer-reviewed publication, achieving the first documented AI-generated paper acceptance. Artifact B analyzes a specific component of autonomous research systems - ablation planning via causal reasoning - and finds that d-separation over-prunes valid ablations, making it unsuitable for experimental design.

agreements.description: Both artifacts work toward automating aspects of ML research processes
Both identify significant limitations in their respective approaches - A requires human selection at multiple stages, B shows d-separation fails for experimental design
Both emphasize the importance of proper evaluation protocols for automated research systems

unique_to_a: First documented AI-generated paper acceptance at peer-reviewed venue
End-to-end paper generation pipeline from ideas to publication
VLM integration for figure quality improvement
33.3% success rate in peer review process
unique_to_b: D-separation over-prunes valid ablations (96% pruning rate, 68% false positives)
Five distinct failure modes of causal reasoning for experimental design
Structural Causal Models for ML system representation
Perfect precision (100%) but low recall (32%) for CausalAP
Causal independence vs experimental redundancy distinction
complementary: A provides proof-of-concept for full research automation while B identifies critical component limitations
A's success validates the potential of automated research systems, while B's findings help improve specific components
B's ablation planning insights could improve A's experimental design stage
A's peer review validation provides real-world testing that B lacks
Together they span system-level evaluation (A) and component-level analysis (B)
methodology_differences: A uses real peer review validation vs B uses expert-annotated ground truth comparison
A evaluates complete end-to-end pipeline vs B focuses on single component (ablation planning)
A has small sample size (N=3) vs B uses larger benchmark (21 real + 50 synthetic systems)
A requires human selection at multiple stages vs B is fully automated
A uses tree search for exploration vs B uses causal reasoning for planning
A targets workshop-level publication vs B targets component optimization
full_result.recommendation: These artifacts are highly complementary. Use A's results to demonstrate the feasibility of automated research systems and B's findings to improve the experimental design components within such systems. A shows what's possible at the system level, while B identifies critical limitations that must be addressed. Future work should integrate B's insights about the limitations of d-separation into A's experimental planning stage, potentially replacing or augmenting the tree search with better causal reasoning approaches that avoid the over-pruning problem identified in B.

evaluator_exp-2025-12-01__vs__evaluator_.summary: These artifacts investigate two different aspects of autonomous AI research systems. Artifact A demonstrates an end-to-end system for automated research paper generation that achieved the first AI-generated paper to pass peer review. Artifact B investigates whether causal reasoning can improve ablation planning for such systems, finding that d-separation over-prunes valid ablations. They are complementary works addressing different stages of the automated research pipeline.

full_result.summary: These artifacts investigate two different aspects of autonomous AI research systems. Artifact A demonstrates an end-to-end system for automated research paper generation that achieved the first AI-generated paper to pass peer review. Artifact B investigates whether causal reasoning can improve ablation planning for such systems, finding that d-separation over-prunes valid ablations. They are complementary works addressing different stages of the automated research pipeline.

agreements.topic: Autonomous AI research systems need improvement
agreements.description: Both artifacts acknowledge limitations in current automated research approaches and work toward improving different components
Both studies have small sample sizes that limit statistical conclusions (n=3 for A, n=21 for B)
Both artifacts operate within the machine learning research domain

unique_to_a: First AI-generated paper to achieve peer review acceptance
Template-free operation enabling broader domain coverage
VLM integration for improved figure quality
End-to-end paper generation and submission workflow
unique_to_b: Causal reasoning approach to ablation planning
Five distinct failure modes of causal methods
Structural Causal Models for experimental design
complementary: Artifact A provides the end-to-end research generation system while B could improve its ablation study component
A demonstrates feasibility of automated research while B provides tools to make such systems more rigorous
A's tree-search exploration could benefit from B's insights about experimental design
B's ablation planning methods could be integrated into A's Stage 4 (Ablation Studies)
Together they address the full pipeline: research generation (A) and experimental validation (B)
methodology_differences: A uses progressive agentic tree-search vs B uses causal reasoning pipelines
A evaluates through actual peer review vs B evaluates against expert annotations
A focuses on paper generation quality vs B focuses on experimental design quality
A tests end-to-end system vs B tests specific component (ablation planning)
A uses multi-agent coordination vs B uses single causal inference pipeline
full_result.recommendation: These artifacts should be viewed as complementary rather than competing approaches. Artifact A demonstrates the feasibility of automated research generation, while Artifact B identifies critical flaws in causal approaches to experimental design that could improve A's ablation study component. Future work should integrate B's insights about d-separation limitations into A's Stage 4 ablation planning, while A's successful peer review achievement validates the broader goal that B's methods aim to support.
